name: Alto Alentejo Portuguese
notes: Alentejano Portuguese (Portalegre, Évora, Beja; Alto Alentejo type). Modelled features: (1) DEFINING zone feature — palatalisation/fronting of the stressed labio-velar /u/ → [y]. Cintra (1971) delimits the Beira-Baixa / Alto-Alentejo zone by exactly this isogloss, ‘a palatalização, em maior ou menor grau, da vogal tónica u’ (p. 14 of the CVC reflow), within ‘uma profunda alteração de timbre de todo o sistema vocálico, principalmente do tónico’; the explicit [y] value is given in Cintra’s adjacent Barlavento-Algarvio passage of the same ‘reacção em cadeia’, and Alto-Alentejo points are recorded with palatalised ‘ü’ in Cintra’s note 56. Encoded as allophone rule ALE_U_FRONTING (stressed nucleus only), with the same PROCLITIC GUARD as pt-PT-x-algarve — the function words o/os/no/nos/do/dos/ao/aos/pelo/pelos/um/uns get word_exceptions so the monosyllabic-clitic /u/ (mis-marked stressed by the word-level detector) is never fronted to [y]. (2) DELETION of final unstressed high vowels -u/-i/-e; encoded as allophone rule ALE_FINAL_HIGH_VOWEL_DELETION (word-final, unstressed; final /ɐ/ from -a is spared, per the source). Primary source: Brissos (2014), acoustic documentation of central-southern EP. Cintra (1971) also mentions it, but only in note 58 (p. 27), where he is summarising Lüdtke (1956/57) — ‘ocupa-se da queda das vogais finais -u, -i (ou -e), outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região’ — expressly noting its isoglosses do not coincide with the zone’s defining feature; it is therefore NOT Cintra’s own main-text diagnostic and must not be cited as such. (3) INTERVOCALIC /d/ deletion (nada→[ˈnaɐ], vida→[ˈviɐ]); positional_graphemes d/intervocalic→[]. (4) /ej/ → [e] and /ow/ → [o] monophthongs (leite→[ˈletɨ], primeiro→[pɼiˈmeɼu]). (5) meu-class /ew/ → [e] (meu→[me]) via the graphemes overlay. (6) Southern vocalism and vowel breaking [ɛ͜ɐ] in some lexical environments (allophones ɛ). (7) Dark coda /l/→[ł] and coda sibilants inherited from the pt-PT base allophone_rules. Both varieties are central-southern and therefore already share the standard’s unstressed reduction (inherited from pt-PT). DIVERGENCE FROM GOLD: the ep_dialects expert set (n=30) transcribes final vowels in full and does not encode the /u/-fronting or the final-vowel deletion, so ALE_U_FRONTING and ALE_FINAL_HIGH_VOWEL_DELETION lower agreement with that small-n gold while being the cited, linguistically-correct realisations — documented, not tuned away.

ancestors.notes: Standard European Portuguese; Alentejo diverges mainly in open vowel quality and transitional seseo-adjacent features near the Spanish border

sources.author: Cintra, L. F. Lindley
sources.title: Nova proposta de classificação dos dialectos galego-portugueses
sources.publisher: Boletim de Filologia (Centro de Estudos Filológicos, Lisboa), 22: 81–116
sources.notes: Foundational classification of European Portuguese dialects. Delimits the Beira-Baixa / Alto-Alentejo zone (and, in an adjacent passage, the Barlavento Algarvio) by the isogloss of stressed-/u/ palatalisation — ‘a palatalização, em maior ou menor grau, da vogal tónica u’ (p. 14 of the CVC reflow) — within ‘uma profunda alteração de timbre de todo o sistema vocálico, principalmente do tónico’; the explicit front-rounded [y] value appears in the Algarvio passage of the same ‘reacção em cadeia’, and Alto-Alentejo survey points are transcribed with palatalised ‘ü’ in note 56. Final unstressed high-vowel deletion is NOT one of Cintra’s own diagnostics: it appears only in his note 58 (p. 27), summarising Lüdtke (1956/57) — ‘ocupa-se da queda das vogais finais -u, -i (ou -e), outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região, mas cujos limites não coincidem perfeitamente com os dos primeiros’ — and is here sourced to Brissos (2014).

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando
sources.title: New insights into Portuguese central-southern dialects: understanding their present and past forms through acoustic data from stressed vowels
sources.notes: Acoustic study of central-southern EP vowel systems. Documents Algarvio stressed /u/ → [y] ([tˈyd] ‘tudo’, [lˈymɨ] ‘lume’) and final unstressed [u] deletion/reduction ([sˈœʃt] ‘cesto’); cites Lüdtke (1957) and Maia (1975–78) that the /u/ palatalisation is stressed-only.

sources.author: Boléo, M.P.
sources.title: Estudos de linguística portuguesa e românica

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam).

allophone_rules.notes: Palatalisation/fronting of the stressed labio-velar /u/ to the front rounded [y] — the feature by which Cintra (1971) delimits the Beira-Baixa / Alto-Alentejo zone: ‘a palatalização, em maior ou menor grau, da vogal tónica u’ (p. 14 of the CVC reflow), part of ‘uma profunda alteração de timbre de todo o sistema vocálico, principalmente do tónico’. Cintra writes the explicit IPA [y] for the neighbouring Barlavento Algarvio of the same ‘reacção em cadeia’ and records the Alto-Alentejo tonic u as palatalised ‘ü’ (note 56); [y] is its phonetic value. Restricted to the STRESSED nucleus (stress=‘stressed’): unstressed /u/ is untouched, so it does not feed on the inherited pt-PT unstressed /o/→[u] reduction. NOTES-ONLY (not modelled): the wider chain shift ([a]→[ɔ]-ward velarisation, [ɔ]→[o], [e]/[ɛ] labialisation) is context-conditioned and left to notes to avoid collapsing the vowel space; only the categorical /u/→[y] is realised. A proclitic guard (see word_exceptions) keeps monosyllabic clitics — o/os/no/nos/do/dos/ao/aos/pelo/pelos/um/uns — on their [u] vowel, since the word-level stress detector mis-marks them as stressed.

Deletion of final unstressed high vowels -u/-i/-e. Primary source: Brissos (2014), acoustic study of central-southern EP (‘final unstressed [u] disappears or is reduced to [ɨ]’, e.g. [sˈœʃt] ‘cesto’). Cintra (1971) mentions it only in note 58 (p. 27), summarising Lüdtke (1956/57) — ‘a queda das vogais finais -u, -i (ou -e), outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região, mas cujos limites não coincidem perfeitamente com os dos primeiros’ — so it is NOT Cintra’s own main-text diagnostic and is not cited as such. In pt-PT the final unstressed vowels surface as [u] (from graphic o/u) and [ɨ] (from e); this rule drops them word-finally (gosto→[ˈɡɔʃt], noite→[ˈnojt]). Conditioned word_final + unstressed so it never touches stressed or word-medial vowels; final /ɐ/ (from -a) is deliberately excluded, matching the sources, which list only final -u, -i, -e. This is a categorical realisation of a variable process; see notes on the divergence from the small-n ep_dialects gold.
